d: To endure a painful or difficult situation bravely
e: "I bit the bullet and told my boss the bad news."

d: To say or do something to reduce tension and ease introductions
e: "He told a joke to break the ice at the meeting."

d: To permanently damage a relationship by your actions
e: "Don't burn bridges — you may need their help later."

d: To suddenly become nervous or hesitant about doing something
e: "He got cold feet just before the big presentation."

w: hit the nail on the head
d: To describe exactly what is causing a situation
e: "You hit the nail on the head with that observation."

d: To miss an opportunity; to be too late
e: "He missed the boat on investing in that startup."
"I only eat fast food once in a blue moon."
"I'm still on the fence about which job to take."
"Who spilled the beans about the surprise party?"

d: The final problem that causes you to give up or react
e: "Being late again was the last straw — she quit."
"I'm feeling a bit under the weather today."
"The meeting time is still up in the air."

d: Something that seems bad but turns out to be good
e: "Losing that job was a blessing in disguise."

w: cost an arm and a leg
e: "That bag costs an arm and a leg."

d: To leave and start a journey
e: "We should hit the road early to beat the traffic."

w: kill two birds with one stone
d: To accomplish two things with a single action
e: "I'll stop by the bank on the way — kill two birds with one stone."

w: let the cat out of the bag
e: "He let the cat out of the bag about the promotion."

d: Said when someone who was just mentioned appears
e: "Speak of the devil — here comes James now."

w: bite off more than you can chew
d: To take on more responsibility than you can handle
e: "I think I bit off more than I can chew with this project."

d: A small visible part of a much larger problem
e: "The errors we found are just the tip of the iceberg."
"I'm exhausted — time to hit the sack."

w: the ball is in your court
d: It is your turn to act
e: "I've done my part; the ball is in your court."

d: To do something cheaply or carelessly
e: "Don't cut corners on safety."
"Relax, I'm just pulling your leg."

d: To start over after a failure
e: "The plan failed, so it's back to the drawing board."
"If you suspect me, you're barking up the wrong tree."
"Stop beating around the bush and tell me."
"Working part-time gives her the best of both worlds."
"The party got out of hand."
"She always goes the extra mile for clients."
"We're all in the same boat here."
"Everyone jumped on the bandwagon."
"Keep training — no pain, no gain."
"She was on cloud nine after the news."
"I hate to rain on your parade, but it's cancelled."
"We don't always see eye to eye."
"Stop sitting on the fence and decide."

w: take with a grain of salt
e: "Take his advice with a grain of salt."
"After months of trying, they threw in the towel."
"I can't wrap my head around these rules."

d: To work late into the night
e: "She burned the midnight oil before the exam."
"Finals are near, time to hit the books."
"Let's cut to the chase."
"He's in hot water with his boss."
"The new assistant is really on the ball."
"I've been out of the loop lately."

d: To use influence to get something
e: "She pulled strings to get the job."
"That name rings a bell."
"Don't steal her thunder at the party."

d: What you do matters more than what you say
e: "He promised a lot, but actions speak louder than words."

d: Simpler to suggest than to do
e: "Quitting sugar is easier said than done."
